coredata.dc:title: A Zn-incorporated PCN-224 combined with smartphone-based colorimetric/fluorescence sensing platform for simultaneous detection of profenofos and glyphosate in traditional Chinese medicinal herbs
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Xiudan
Wang, Zirou
Yu, Hui
Liu, Yufei
Yang, Qingli
coredata.dc:description: Excess residues of organophosphorus pesticides in traditional Chinese medicinal herbs has been driving the development of rapid and specific detection methods. Herein, a dual-mode sensing platform was established for the simultaneous determination of glyphosate and profenofos using the colorimetric and fluorescence sensors, respectively. A Zn-incorporated porous coordination network-224 (PCN-224) was prepared to selectively adsorb glyphosate based on the coordination effect of Zr and phosphate ion, which would inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of PCN-224(Zn). An aptamer-modified nitrogen-carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was used to specifically recognize profenofos, further adopting the quenching effect of PCN-224(Zn) towards N-CQDs to realize the quantitative analysis. The colorimetric sensing mechanism was also studied. Coupled with a smartphone application, the established method exhibited the linearity of 0.06–3.1 μg mL−1 (glyphosate), and 0.02–3.9 μg mL−1 (profenofos). The matrix effect was 100.58 % and 95.46 % in Angelica sinensis and Codonopsis, presenting a new horizon for the multi-model sensor in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organophosphorus pesticides
Metal-organic framework
Colorimetric sensor
Fluorescence sensor
Chinese medicinal herbs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-mode sensing of glyphosate and profenofos

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Rural Revitalization Science and Technology Innovation Plan Project of Shandong Province
Qingdao science and technology Huimin demonstration special project
National College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the financial support from Shandong Higher Education \u201CYouth Innovation Team Plan\u201D (2023KJ165), Postgraduate innovation gram of Qingdao Agricultural University (QNYCX23042), National College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202310435196), Rural Revitalization Science and Technology Innovation Plan Project of Shandong Province (2022TZXD0031), Qingdao science and technology Huimin demonstration special project (24-1-8-xdny-15-nsh) and Central guidance for local scientific and technological development special funds (24-1-4-5-zyyd-nsh).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation of PCN-224(Zn); (B) Construction of PCN-224(Zn) combined with smartphone-based colorimetric/fluorescence sensing platform.

(A) TEM image of PCN-224(Zn); (B-F) EDS-element mappings of PCN-224(Zn) corresponding to C, N, O, Zr and Zn. (G) Particle size distribution of PCN-224(Zn). XRD patterns (H), FTIR spectra (I) of different materials. (J) XPS of PCN-224(Zn). The deconvoluted spectra of Zn2p (K) and N1s (L) in PCN-224(Zn). (M−N) AFM images of N-CQDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of different systems. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of PCN-224(Zn) under the substrates of ABTS and OPD. Effect of (C) pH and (D) temperature on the catalytic activity of PCN-224(Zn). (E) The relative activity of PCN-224(Zn) in 20 days. Steady-state kinetic curves of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) toward (F) TMB. The corresponding double-reciprocal plots of (G) TMB. (H) K cat and K cat-specific diagram of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) nanospheres.

(A) Effects of various active scavengers. (B) ESR spectra for •OH detection. FTIR spectra (C), XPS survey spectra (D) and high-resolution spectra of P2p (E), N1s (F), O1s (G) and Zr3d (H) in PCN-224(Zn) before and after interacting with GLP.

(A-B) Optimization of incubation time and pH in PCN-224(Zn) colorimetric system. (C) The linear relationship between GLP concentration and absorbance value. (D) Schematics of the smartphone for the GLP detection and linear plot of color intensity (B/G) vs GLP concentration.

(A-C) Optimization of materials concentration, incubation time and pH value in PCN-224(Zn) fluorescence system. The fluorescent spectra (D) and linear fitting curve (E) of fluorescent mode. (F-I) Specificity and anti-interference ability of the established method.
Detection of glyphosate in real samples.
Detection of profenofos in real samples.

head.ce:title: A Zn-incorporated PCN-224 combined with smartphone-based colorimetric/fluorescence sensing platform for simultaneous detection of profenofos and glyphosate in traditional Chinese medicinal herbs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jimo Comprehensive Inspection and Testing Center, Qingdao 266200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jimo Comprehensive Inspection and Testing Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jimo Comprehensive Inspection and Testing Center, Qingdao 266200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excess residues of organophosphorus pesticides in traditional Chinese medicinal herbs has been driving the development of rapid and specific detection methods. Herein, a dual-mode sensing platform was established for the simultaneous determination of glyphosate and profenofos using the colorimetric and fluorescence sensors, respectively. A Zn-incorporated porous coordination network-224 (PCN-224) was prepared to selectively adsorb glyphosate based on the coordination effect of Zr and phosphate ion, which would inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of PCN-224(Zn). An aptamer-modified nitrogen-carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was used to specifically recognize profenofos, further adopting the quenching effect of PCN-224(Zn) towards N-CQDs to realize the quantitative analysis. The colorimetric sensing mechanism was also studied. Coupled with a smartphone application, the established method exhibited the linearity of 0.06–3.1 μg mL−1 (glyphosate), and 0.02–3.9 μg mL−1 (profenofos). The matrix effect was 100.58 % and 95.46 % in Angelica sinensis and Codonopsis, presenting a new horizon for the multi-model sensor in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) are commonly used to kill insects and improve the quality and yield of traditional Chinese medicinal herbs. OPPs are a kind of irreversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, so the residual OPPs would pollute the environment and cause health risks [1,2]. The excessive use would enable them to accumulate in Chinese traditional medicinal crops and further enter into the human body to damage the nervous system [3,4]. Traditional detection methods include high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, etc. [5,6]. These techniques need expensive instruments and skilled operators and can not to meet the demand for the on-site detection. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a rapid, convenient, simple and convenient sensing platform to monitor OPPs before and after picking medicinal herbs in the field.

In various methods, the colorimetric sensing analysis is potential due to the easy manipulation, low cost and visual readout [7–9]. The method sensitivity and specificity depend on the peroxidase, oxidase-like activity of nanozymes. As a large class of porous crystal materials, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have the advantages of high porosity, adjustable composition or structure, good stability, which can be used for the construction of various sensing platforms [10–12]. Recently, MOFs with an intrinsic enzyme-like activity have developed and applied in colorimetric sensors, such as MIL-88 [13], Co-MOF [14], Ni-MOF [15], Cu-MOF [16]. Porous coordination network-224 (PCN-224) is a porphyrin Zr-MOF with stable structure and easy synthesized strategy. The porphyrin cavity of PCN-224 provides an active site for the modification of some metals to bring in a heme-like structure, such as PCN-224-Mn [17], Au@PCN-224 [18], Ag@PCN-224 [19]. Meanwhile, their enzyme-like properties are also improved. Besides, the Zr atoms can form Zr-O-P bonds with the phosphate groups. Luo et al. developed a Fe-doped Zr-MOF nanosensor for the specific recognition and detection of glyphosate [20]. The large surface area of MOF nanosheets endowed them powerful preconcentration capacity towards the target compounds, and the Zr6O8 clusters also served as specific binding sites for selective identification of glyphosate. Therefore, it is feasible that a metal-incorporated PCN-224 based on the colorimetric sensing platform can be constructed for the detection of glyphosate.

Simultaneous detection of multiple pesticides is a challenging subject due to the complexity and diversity [21]. In recent years, a multiple-signal integrated platform has been developed to improve the detection sensitivity [22–24]. A dual-signal integrated platform based on the combination of colorimetric signal with other sensing modes may be an alluring protocol to realize the simultaneous determination of two OPPs. The ligand (tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin, H2TCPP) in PCN-224 is a typical fluorescence quencher which can realize the fluorescence resonance energy transfer [25]. Fluorescence sensors with high sensitivity and accuracy are easy-to-operation, portable. Colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensing can be established due to the visual signal of colorimetric and high sensitivity of fluorescence [26–29]. Among the fluorescent probes, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great attention in biosensing due to the facile synthesis and good biocompatibility [30–32]. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) possess more active sites, high optical activity and chemical stability [33–35]. Besides, the homogeneous crystalline hybrid MOF has a large specific surface area, which is also suitable for anchoring RNA, DNA, and aptamers [36–40]. Therefore, with the help of specific identification of aptamer and fluorescence signal probe, the colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode platform can detect multiple analytes.

In this work, a colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode sensing platform based on the PCN-224(Zn) and N-CQDs was constructed for the simultaneous determination of glyphosate and profenofos. The physical properties and successful synthesis of PCN-224(Zn) and N-CQDs were confirmed via a series of characterizations. Their colorimetric and fluorescence properties were investigated through the model plotting. Coupled with a smartphone application, the established method was used to detect glyphosate and profenofos in two traditional Chinese medicinal herbs.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) images were acquired from JSM-6701S (Hitachi, Japan), JEM-2100F (Hitachi, Japan), SPM-9700 (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on a ESCALAB Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was obtained from X-ray diffraction (Bruker, D8 Focus, Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation. The optical properties were characterized using the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet IS10, Nicolet, USA). Mini-Sub-Cell gel electrophoresis (GT system, Bio-Rad Laboratory, North Carolina）was used to verify the linkage of aptamer on N-CQDs. The active substances were detected by electron spin resonance (ESR) (Bruker ESR E500, Germany). The UV spectra and absorbance values were measured by a spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific). The fluorescence spectra were obtained from on a RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan).

ZrOCl2·8H2O, H2TCPP, ZnCl2, benzoic acid, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), Glyphosate, Profenofos, Tryptophan (Trp) and Superoxide dismutase (SOD) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). NaAc, H2O2, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), urea, isopropyl alcoholand (IPA), citric acid were obtained from Shanghai Guoyao Reagent Group (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and N-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl)-N-ethyl-carbodiimide (EDC) were bought from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (OPD) was purchased from Beijing Yaanda Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2,2′-Diazo­benzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) was obtained from Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hefei, China).
The aptamer sequence was synthesized in Sangon Company (Shanghai, China), which was modified with –NH2 at the 3′-end.
Aptamer: 5′-AGCTTGCTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGCCACAGAGCT-NH2-3′.

PCN-224 was prepared via a simple solvothermal method. Specifically, 120 mg of ZrOCl2·8H2O, 30 mg of H2TCPP and 1.6 g of benzoic acid were added into 8 mL of DMF, sonicating for 10 min. After that, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 120℃. After 12 h, the precipitate was separated and washed repeatedly with 100 mL DMF for 3 times, and then dried to obtain PCN-224 powder.

PCN-224(Zn) was synthesized according to the previously reported method [41]. Firstly, 20 mg of PCN-224 were added to 10 mL of DMF. Then, 38 mg of ZnCl2 was added to the DMF solution. The mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 120℃ for 12 h to obtain the corresponding PCN-224(Zn). Thereafter, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed repeatedly with DMF, and dried to obtain PCN-224(Zn).

Synthesis of N-CQDs: 0.40 g of urea and 1.06 g of citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for reacting at 160 °C. After 4 h, the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature, and put into a 3500 Da dialysis bag. The collected solution outside the dialysis bag was dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag to remove unreacted small molecules to obtain pure N-CQDs, which were stored in the dark at 4 °C.
Binding of N-CQDs and aptamer: 5 μL EDC, 10 μL NHS, 10 μL of aptamer and 30 μL of N-CQDs were incubated at 25℃ for 12 h, and stored at 4℃.

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) were studied in different systems with TMB, OPD, and ABTS as substrates. In detail, 40 μL of PCN-224 or PCN-224(Zn) (2.5 mg mL−1) was mixed with 160 μL of HAC/NaAc buffer (pH = 3.5), and 80 μL of H2O2 and 40 μL of (TMB, OPD, or ABTS) were added into the catalytic system incubating for 5 min at room temperature. The absorbance at a wavelength of 652 nm (A652) was observed as an indicator of peroxidase activity. Steady-state kinetic studies were conducted under different concentrations of TMB and H2O2 under the certain concentration of TMB or H2O2. The related parameters were determined and plotted via Michaelis-Menten equation (Eq. (1) and Lineweaver Burk equation (Eq. (2).
(1)

=
V
m ax

+[ S]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode sensing of glyphosate and profenofos
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of glyphosate: 40 μL of PCN-224(Zn) (2.5 mg L−1) was added into 80 μ L of varied concentrations of glyphosate in 1 mL of HAc/NaAc buffer (pH = 3.5), incubating at room temperature for 5 min. Afterward, 40 μL of TMB (10 mM) and 80 μL of H2O2 (100 mM) were added into the above system to react at 35 °C for 5 min. The solution color was recorded by a smartphone “ColorPicker” application, and the UV–vis absorbance spectra at A652 was recorded.

Detection of profenofos: 10 μL of N-CQDs-aptamer fluorescence probe was added to a 20 μL of profenofos solution, incubating at 25 °C for 2 h. Then, 20 μL of PCN-224(Zn) (2.5 mg L−1) was added to the mixture and incubated at 25 °C for 2 h. The supernatant was collected and exposed to the fluorescence determination (Fig. 1 ).

The roots of Codonopsis pilosula and Angelica sinensis (Dingxi, China) were cut into small pieces for homogenization at ethanol/water (8:2, v/v) solution for 30 min. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to be tested.

The microstructure of the prepared PCN-224(Zn) was characterized using TEM. Fig. 2 (A-G) showed that the framework of PCN-224(Zn) included Zr6 clusters connected with square plane TCPP ligands. Zr6(OH)8 was the core and connected with six TCPP ligands, and the μ3-OH group covered the triangular plane. PCN-224(Zn) exhibited the concave cubic shape with a cross-sectional diameter of approximately 200 nm, and the cubic crystals possessed some bumps, hollows and rugged features. Besides, quite a few Zn nanoparticles were observed on the outer surface of PCN-224(Zn). It also described a lattice fringe and spatial distribution of elements, which further demonstrated the existential states of main elements (C, N, O, Zr, Zn) in PCN-224(Zn). The particle size distribution was approximately 70–290 nm, and most concentrated around 190 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2H, the XRD patterns of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) possessed a consistent diffraction pattern. The significant characteristic diffraction peaks at 4.5°, 6.3°, 8.2°, 11.2°, and 13.82° were corresponding to the (002), (022), (222), (004), (224) and (006) lattice planes of PCN-224, respectively. And the modification of Zn would not change the pristine crystalline structure of PCN-224.

The functional groups of PCN-224 and PCN-224 (Zn) were further verified via FT-IR. Fig. 2I showed that PCN-224 and PCN-224 (Zn) possessed asymmetric vibrational absorptions at 1246 cm−1 (C-OH) and 1653 cm−1 (C=O) from the carboxylic acid group in H2TCPP. Compared with PCN-224, the N-H adsorption (around 3500 cm−1) was weakening, and a strong symmetrical Zn-N stretching peak at 1021 cm−1 appeared, indicating the incomplete metallization of porphyrin ring to form the porphyrin-metal composite.

The chemical composition and state of PCN-224(Zn) was investigated by XPS, which was a useful tool for determining the chemical state and related elemental composition of nanomaterials. Fig. 2J showed that PCN-224(Zn) was composed of C, N, O, Zr and Zn elements. Besides, the characteristic Zn peaks corresponding to 1022.60 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1022.60 eV (Zn 2p3/2) were observed in Fig. 2K, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum of PCN-224(Zn) was also divided into two nitrogen-based peaks in Fig. 2L. The above characterizations all proved the successful preparation of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) and demonstrated their similarity and distinction in compositions and structures.

The morphology and structure of N-CQDs were characterized using AFM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2(M, N), N-CQDs with good dispersibility were observed in the AFM image. The particle size was around 8 nm. Fig. S1 showed the TEM image of N-CQDs, which possessed the nanoparticles of ∼ 8 nm size, according to the AFM image. Meanwhile, the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs was also verified in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2I). The peak at 3500–3200 cm−1 was corresponding to the stretching vibration peak of N-H and O-H bonds. The stretching vibration peaks of C=C and C=O were 1580 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1, respectively. It presented the abundant hydrophilic hydroxyl, carboxyl groups and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs.

To further verify the modification of aptamer on N-CQDs, the concentration of aptamer before and after the reaction with N-CQDs was determined by a gel electrophoresis. As shown in Fig. S2, the pure aptamer in Lane 1 before reacting with N-CQDs exhibited the deeper staining. Lane 2 representing the residual aptamer after reacting with N-CQDs became obviously shallower. It indicated that most aptamers were successfully connected with N-CQDs and a few aptamers remained in the suspension, which were used for the specific recognition for profenofos.

The catalytic performance of PCN-224(Zn) was investigated using TMB as the substrate. As shown in Fig. 3 A, PCN-224(Zn) exhibited a significant color change in the TMB/H2O2 system, from colorless to blue, with a clear absorbance at 652 nm, while the individual system had no absorbance. The bright blue color in PCN-224(Zn)-TMB/H2O2 system exhibited the successful oxidation reaction of TMB to oxTMB, indicating PCN-224 (Zn) possessed the excellent peroxidase-like activity. The catalytic ability of PCN-224(Zn) towards other chromogenic substrates (ABTS and OPD) in the presence of H2O2 was also studied and analyzed. In the OPD/H2O2 system with HAc/NaAc (pH = 3.0) buffer, the reaction solution changed from colorless to yellow and there was a clear absorption peak at 450 nm (Fig. 3B). In the ABTS/H2O2 system with HAc/NaAc (pH = 3.0) buffer, the color of solution significantly darkened. The catalytic oxidation of other two chromogenic substrates of ABTS and OPD in the presence of H2O2 further verified the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of PCN-224(Zn). However, the OPD/H2O2 system needed to be carried out under dark conditions, and ABST/H2O2 system had relatively low absorbance. Considering the high sensitivity and stability of TMB, it was chosen as the chromogenic substrate.

The enzyme activity of nanomaterials is closely related to environmental pH and temperature. Environmental pH would affect the existence state of analytes and TMB. As shown in Fig. 3C, PCN-224 (Zn) exhibited the highest peroxidase-like activity at pH 3.5. Besides, reacting temperature also had a significant impact on the catalytic performance. The UV–vis absorption value of PCN-224 (Zn) catalyzing TMB/H2O2 achieved highest at 35 ℃, indicating that 35 ℃ was the optimal reaction temperature for the catalytical reaction (Fig. 3D). Furthermore, Fig. 3E showed that the peroxidase-like activity of PCN-224 (Zn) displayed an insignificant decrease (relative activity ＞90 %) during 20 days at room temperature, implying its superior stability. Results presented that the as-prepared PCN-224 (Zn) possessed excellent peroxidase-like activity and high stability, which could be developed for a rapid colorimetric sensing platform.

Steady-state kinetic studies were performed by investigating the peroxidase-like catalytic performance of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) at different concentrations of TMB and H2O2, which were plotted with the Michaelis-Menten curve to obtain and compare the related parameters of maximum reaction velocity (V max) and Michaelis constant (K m). It can be observed from that both catalytic curves of TMB and H2O2 using PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn) were hyperbolic, which were consistent with the trends of Michaelis-Menten reactions (Fig. 3F and Fig. S3A). Furthermore, double reciprocal plots were obtained by fitting 1/V vs 1/TMB or 1/H2O2 concentration (Fig. 3G and Fig. S3B). The K m values of PCN-224(Zn) towards TMB and H2O2 were 0.15 and 4.28 mM, which were all lower than those of PCN-224, indicating the better affinity of PCN-224(Zn) towards TMB and H2O2. The V max values of PCN-224(Zn) towards TMB and H2O2 were 6.54 × 10−8 M s−1 and 4.01 × 10−8 M s−1, which were higher than those of PCN-224, implying the more rapid reaction rate and the higher catalytic rate. The K cat (catalytic constant, V max/[S]) value was obtained on the basis of the obtained kinetic parameters. The K cat of PCN-224 (Zn) was as high as 2.62 × 106 M S−1, which was obviously higher than that of PCN-224 (K cat = 1.96 × 106 M s−1) and horseradish peroxide (K cat = 4.30 × 103 M s−1) [42]. The K cat-specific was obtained by normalizing the surface area of K cat being relative to a single nanosphere. As shown in Fig. 3H, it was observed that the trend of K cat-specific was similar to that of K cat. Table S1 also compared some parameters (V max, K cat, K cat-specific) of PCN-224, PCN-224(Zn) and other reported nanozymes, presenting the relatively superior catalytic performance of PCN-224 and PCN-224(Zn).

The catalytic mechanism of PCN-224(Zn) towards TMB in the presence of H2O2 was investigated and studied. Some previous researches reported the redox electron cycling from metal centers with different valence states was critical to the catalytic activity of metal-doped MOF nanozymes, which would generate different types of reactive oxygen radicals (ROS) [41]. The radical scavenging experiment was carried out using three radical quenchers with different concentrations, including Trp for 1O2, SOD for •O2 radical, and IPA for •OH radical, respectively. It can be observed from Fig. 4 A that the peroxidase-like activity of PCN-224(Zn) would display a downward trend after the addition of three radical quenchers. The absorbance would obviously decreased after the addition of IPA, indicating the dominating participation of •OH radicals during the catalytic process. Furthermore, the formation of •OH in the reaction system was investigated using the ESR spectroscopy, where DMPO was used as the scavenger for radicals. As shown in Fig. 4B, there were also characteristic peaks (1:2:2:1) corresponding to the typical •OH signal.

The adsorption/interaction between PCN-224(Zn) and glyphosate was speculated as a reasonable inhibition mechanism, which was investigated via the characterization of FT-IR and XPS. The FT-IR spectra was presented in Fig. 4C. The decrease of Zr-O peak at 652 cm−1 proved the participation of Zr-O bond in the adsorption of glyphosate. Besides, the peaks between 1550 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 generated from C=O vibrations changed after the adsorption of glyphosate, implying that the C=O groups such as carboxyl were also involved in the recognition of glyphosate. Fig. 4D showed XPS characterizations of PCN-224(Zn) before and after incubating with glyphosate to further demonstrate the host–guest interactions between them. As shown in Fig. 4E, the new characteristic peak of P2p appeared at 133 eV in PCN-224(Zn) after capturing glyphosate. Furthermore, the P2p peak with high-resolution was split into two peaks at 132.6 eV and 133.1 eV, corresponding to the P=O bond in glyphosate and the formed Zr-O-P bond between PCN-224(Zn) and glyphosate, respectively. Fig. 4F showed that the appeared N1s of PCN-224(Zn) was fitted with three peaks of N-H (401.7 eV), C=N (399.6 eV), and C-N (397.7 eV). After incubating with glyphosate, the peak of N-H weakened, indicating the decrease of N electron density due to the formation of hydrogen bonding between PCN-224(Zn) and glyphosate. Fig. 4G also showed the peaks of C-O and C=O in O1s spectra shifted before and after the glyphosate capture via the hydrogen bonding interaction. A new peak of Zr-O=P can be observed at 532.3 eV in the O1s spectra of PCN-224(Zn) in the presence of glyphosate, resulting from the surface complexation between Zr and glyphosate to form the Zr-O-P/P=O bonds (Fig. 4H). Similarly, in Zr3d spectra, a new peak of Zr-O-P/P=O at 190.9 eV was appeared, indicating that Zr-O clusters in PCN-224(Zn) could act as tentacles to interact with glyphosate. Also, the Zr3d spectra shifted to higher binding energies. Therefore, multiple physical interactions (electrostatic interaction and hydrogen-bonding) as well as well as Zr-O-P/P=O chemical interactions enhanced the adsorption capacity of PCN-224(Zn) towards glyphosate. The specific or selective adsorption would enable glyphosate to occupy the active sites of PCN-224(Zn) and further hinder the catalytic decomposition of H2O2, which would realize the detection of glyphosate.

Reacting conditions of the colorimetric sensor were studied including solution pH and adsorption time towards glyphosate. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the absorbance would decrease until the adsorption time of PCN-224(Zn) towards glyphosate arrived at 5 min. The solution pH would affect the existing state of glyphosate and the surface charge of PCN-224(Zn). As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorbance gradually increased with the increase of pH = 3–6, and reached the high peak at pH 4. According to pKa of GLP (0.78, 2.29, 5.96 and 10.98), it was existed as an anionic state in the solution of pH = 4.0.

Under optimized experimental conditions, a colorimetric sensing platform was constructed to achieve rapid validation of glyphosate, and a methodological evaluation was established. Fig. 5C showed the linear relationship between the glyphosate concentration and UV–vis absorbance. As the concentration of glyphosate increased, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually decreased. The linear relationship was good within the range of 0.06–3.1 μg mL−1, and the linear regression equation was Y = -0.0446X + 0.2206 (R2 = 0.99681). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.0036μg mL−1. The smartphone with portability has been widely used in the fields of environment and food safety for the on-site monitoring of pollutants. The smartphone camera captured images of different concentrations of glyphosate, and the feature would further automatically extracted by a smartphone software converting the solution color to the RGB value. Fig. 5D fitted the linear relationship with the concentration of glyphosate and the Blue/Green (B/G) value. With the addition of glyphosate, B/G value was linearly related to glyphosate in the range of 0.06–3.1 μg mL−1, according with the equation of Y = -0.0275X + 0.0110 (R2 = 0.9976). Results indicated that the portable colorimetric sensor based on PCN-224 (Zn) would have great potential for the on-site determination of glyphosate.

The detected conditions of profenofos using the fluorescence model were also optimized including the amount of quencher (PCN-224(Zn)), time and pH, and results were presented in Fig. 6 (A–C). As the concentration of PCN-224(Zn) increased from 0.5 mg mL−1 to 4.0 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity continued to increase, and reached the maximum value at 2.5 mg mL−1, indicating the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency at this concentration. Therefore, 2.5 mg mL−1 of PCN-224(Zn) was selected as the optimal concentration for the method establishment. The incubation time between profenofos and N-CQDs-aptamer was closely related to the degree of specific binding affecting the method sensitivity. Before 2 h, the binding of aptamers and profenofos was incomplete, and excess aptamers were adsorbed on PCN-224(Zn) resulting in a low fluorescence signal. Besides, the acidic and basic conditions greatly affect the fluorescent intensity, resulting from the effect of them on the structural change and even hydrolysis of aptamers. So, pH = 7 was selected as the optimal condition.

In accordance with the above optimal conditions, the fluorescence response towards profenofos was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6D, with the concentration of profenofos increasing (0.02–3.9μg mL−1), the fluorescence intensity at 517 nm increased in the normalized fluorescence spectrum. Fig. 6E showed a great linear response between the fluorescence intensity and the profenofos concentration, and the linear equation was Y = 1012.5236X + 932.8676 with R2 = 0.9982. Moreover, LOD was calculated to be 0.0067μg mL−1. Compared with other previous method, this sensor also exhibited relatively lower LOD and higher sensitivity (Table S2).

The selectivity and specificity of the PCN-224(Zn)/N-CQDs-Apt colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode sensing platform were verified by evaluating and comparing its response towards glyphosate, profenofos and other pesticides. It could be observed from Fig. 6F that only glyphosate could obviously inhibit the peroxidase-like activity pf PCN-224(Zn) and further cause the significant decrease of absorbance at 652 nm. In the presence of profenofos, the fluorescent intensity was obviously higher than that of other pesticides (Fig. 6G). Results displayed that this dual-mode sensor exhibited high selectivity towards glyphosate and profenofos. As shown in Fig. 6(H, I), there was no obvious catalytical inhibition and fluorescent intensity change after the addition of other interferences, demonstrating the good anti-interference ability.

In this study, a versatile dual-mode sensing platform was developed using PCN-224(Zn) as the mimicking peroxidase and N-CQDs with the fluorescence property to realize the sensitive, visual and portable detection of glyphosate and profenofos via multidimensional readouts. The determination of glyphosate was completed based on the colorimetric sensing strategy through the inhibition of its peroxidase-like activity towards PCN-224(Zn). The determination of profenofos was realized based on the fluorescence quenching of PCN-224(Zn) towards N-CQDs-aptamer. These signals could be readout by a UV–vis spectrometer, fluorescence spectrophotometer and a smartphone software. The established method was used to detect glyphosate and profenofos in Angelica sinensis and Codonopsis, presenting desirable ME values of 95.46 % to 100.58 % and 90.05 % to 99.00 %. To summarize, this research illuminated a promising reliable, sensitive, portable method to detect pesticides in real samples.

Xiudan Hou: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zirou Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Hui Yu: Validation, Methodology. Yufei Liu: Validation, Methodology. Qingli Yang: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the financial support from Shandong Higher Education “Youth Innovation Team Plan” (2023KJ165), Postgraduate innovation gram of Qingdao Agricultural University (QNYCX23042), National College Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202310435196), Rural Revitalization Science and Technology Innovation Plan Project of Shandong Province (2022TZXD0031), Qingdao science and technology Huimin demonstration special project (24-1-8-xdny-15-nsh) and Central guidance for local scientific and technological development special funds (24-1-4-5-zyyd-nsh).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113546.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced colorimetric detection of norovirus using in-situ growth of Ag shell on Au NPs
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.M. Khoris, K. Takemura, J. Lee, T. Hara, F. Abe, T. Suzuki, E.Y. Park, Enhanced colorimetric detection of norovirus using in-situ growth of Ag shell on Au NPs, Biosens. Bioelectron. 126 (2019) 425-432. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2018.10.067.
